title: Research
text: My research lies at the intersection of formal semantics and syntax. I am particularly interested in how modal meaning relates to the syntactic properties of complements.

title: Research
text:
Modality and complement structure
I investigate the relationship between modal flavor and the syntactic properties of complements. I am especially interested in how epistemic and non-epistemic interpretations interact with complement structure.
My broader goal is to understand how syntactic structure constrains the interpretation of modal expressions.

title: Research
text:
Modal ellipsis and anaphora
My current work examines modal ellipsis and anaphora in French. I use these phenomena to investigate the relationship between modal flavor, complement structure, and the interpretation of unpronounced material.
My approach is primarily theoretical, complemented by experimental work.

Selected work

 Chamot, V. (2026). French Modal Ellipsis and Anaphora: Testing the Epistemic Restriction. Poster presented at Experiments in Linguistic Meaning 4 (ELM 4). [abstract]

title: Research
text:
Experimental work
I use experimental evidence to evaluate formal claims about modality and ellipsis. My current work includes an acceptability-judgment experiment with 213 participants testing the proposed epistemic restriction on French modal ellipsis and anaphora.

title: Victor Chamot
text:
Victor Chamot

    
      PhD Student in Linguistics at the University of Maryland
    

    

      
        I’m a linguistics graduate student at the University of Maryland,
        working on formal semantics and syntax. My main research examines the
        relationship between modal flavor and the syntactic
        properties of complements. I study modal ellipsis
        because I believe it provides insight into this relationship. My
        approach is mainly theoretical, complemented by experimental work.
      

      
        I also have strong interests in logic, philosophy, psycholinguistics,
        and neurolinguistics, and I am pursuing the NACS certificate alongside
        my PhD. I have a longstanding interest in literature too, mostly
        through listening to friends who chose that other path in studying
        language.
      

      
        I’m advised by
        Valentine Hacquard
        and
        Alexander Williams,
        and was previously advised in Geneva by
        Isabelle Charnavel
        and
        Anouk Dieuleveut.
        I am also grateful to
        Utku Türk
        and
        David Müller
        for their invaluable help.

Victor Chamot
PhD Student in Linguistics
University of Maryland
 Advised by Valentine Hacquard and Alexander Williams 
 Formal semantics, syntax, modality, and ellipsis, using theoretical and experimental approaches. 
 Previously at the University of Geneva, where I worked with Isabelle Charnavel and Anouk Dieuleveut. 
chamot@umd.edu
